content.full_text:
1355 42nd Street
Sacramento, CA 95819
February 26, 1991

Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Sir/Madam:

I would appreciate a written opinion addressing a question
which relates to the "revolving door" provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

I have attached a recent letter (January 31, 1991) from Mr.
John McLean, Counsel to the FPPC Enforcement Division. That
letter examines issues regarding the definition of the term
"proceeding" with reference to different actions taken by
the California Pollution Control Financing Authority (CPCFA)
as it monitors and authorizes the issuance of tax-exempt bonds.
In his letter, Mr. McLean concluded that certain official
actions of the California Pollution Control Financing Authority
constitute separate proceedings becuase they (the Initial
Resolution and the Final Resolution) "appear to involve separate
decisions, separate hearings, and most likely consideration
of separate issues." Several advice letters were cited to
support this line of reasoning.

In the above context, it was determined that I did not violate
the "revolving door" provisions of the Political Reform Act
by working with a company called Thermo Electron Corporation.
That opinion applies to activities involving one subsidiary
named the Delano Energy Company, Inc., which had proceedings
before the CPCFA. It was determined that I did not assist
Thermo Electron or its Delano project in the same proceedings
as those which occurred during the time I served as Executive
Officer of the CPCFA. It appears to me that the same reasoning
also applies to proceedings affecting the North County Resource
Recovery Associates (NCRRA) project in San Diego (another
subsidiary of Thermo). Based on the principles and precedents
cited in Mr. McLean's letter, I believe it is self-evident
that future actions which may be taken by CPCFA regarding
the NCRRA project do not constitute the same proceedings as
those in which I was involved while I served as Executive
Officer of the CPCFA in 1988 and earlier. Please confirm that
my understanding is accurate.

By way of background, NCRRA is a separate subsidiary of Thermo
Electron. While I was Executive Officer of CPCFA, the NCRRA
project was considered at several separate proceedings. In
November 1985, CPCFA issued this company a Final Resolution
approving the issuance of bonds to be placed "in escrow" for

a period of up to one year only. Under the terms applied
by the CPCFA, the bonds would collapse and be redeemed if
NCRRA could not meet certain obligations within the one-year
deadline. Later, a different agency, the California Debt
Limit Allocation Committee (CDLAC), awarded bond allocation
to NCRRA during one of its own separate proceedings. At the
end of one year, in November 1986, NCRRA requested that the
CPCFA schedule a new hearing to consider a different course
of action. NCRRA could not meet the original conditions
which would have permitted release of bond funds from escrow.
At this hearing, NCRRA raised new issues in support of its
new request for subsequent bonds that would replace those
which would be rescinded under the previous conditions. After
a roll call vote, the CPCFA decided to call the old bonds
and issue new ones. Another separate action was also taken
during proceedings in November 1987. In November 1988, following
lengthy testimony over new issues and new litigation, the
CPCFA approved a subsequent resolution creating a different
escrow structure with a new set of conditions which would
have permitted break of escrow at some time during the next
two years. Early the next year, in February of 1989, I resigned
my position and established a private consulting business.

During the following two years, NCRRA was unable to meet the
set of conditions outlined in the resolution approved in
November 1988 proceeding. Consequently, in November 1990,
NCRRA applied to the CPCFA for a new public hearing and requested
a separate proceeding to consider another course of action.
Once again, CPCFA had the disretion to do nothing, to vote
against another restructuring, or to design and impose yet
another escrow arrangement with different conditions, caveats
and deadlines. After considering new issues and pursuant
to a new roll call vote at this proceeding (and under a new
chairman with a new Executive Officer) CPCFA approved a new
resolution mandating a distinct set of conditions and authorizing
new "escrow bonds." After the resolution was adopted, the
old bonds were called and new bonds were issued. New reoffering
circulars were prepared and distributed to purchasers. New
letters of credit backing the bonds had to be secured.

NCRRA is now considering several possible future courses of
action. They are contemplating the possibility of applying
for CPCFA approval to actually issue bonds, or they may apply
for new bond allocation from CDLAC, or they may apply for
another escrow restructuring, or they may seek a combination
of the above alternatives. Any of the above will require
a separate discretionary action of the CPCFA (or CDLAC or
both) together with completely new and distinct proceedings.
All of these would be separate from any earlier proceedings
in which I had a participating role. Moreover, the composition
of the CPCFA again changed in January 1991. A new State
Treasurer chairs the CPCFA and a new Executive Officer has
been appointed.

I hope it is obvious that the current restructured escrow
financing for NCRRA, created by CPCFA in November 1990, is
not one in which I participated in any manner by making a
staff recommendation or otherwise. In his letter of January
31, Mr. McLean correctly noted that my work for Thermo Electron
involved only the Delano subsidiary and the Delano project.
I do not presently have any business relationship with Thermo
Electron or either of the subsidiaries mentioned in this letter.
However, it is my understanding that there would be no violation
of the Act's "revolving door" provisions if I were to enter
a consulting relationship to assist Thermo Electron in shaping
future bond financing strategies for the NCRRA project. I
believe this understanding is supported by the line of reasoning
and the precedents contained in Mr. McLean's letter.

Please confirm that I properly understand the application
of the term "proceeding" in this instance. It may be helpful
to review an earlier letter to the Legal Division (November
14, 1990) in which I describe different CPCFA proceedings.
While this specific request was not answered, Mr. McLean's
letter addresses many of the critical issues. I was informed
that these requests for written opinions can be answered in
twenty-one days. I would appreciate an early reply. I have
tried to include as much useful information as possible, but
please do not hesitate to call me at (916) 452-1381 if I can
clarify matters in any way.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Douglas E. Chandler

sections.question: Please confirm that my understanding is accurate [regarding whether future actions by CPCFA regarding the NCRRA project constitute the same proceedings as those in which I was involved while serving as Executive Officer of CPCFA in 1988 and earlier]. It is my understanding that there would be no violation of the Act's 'revolving door' provisions if I were to enter a consulting relationship to assist Thermo Electron in shaping future bond financing strategies for the NCRRA project. Please confirm that I properly understand the application of the term 'proceeding' in this instance.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks clarification on whether future CPCFA proceedings regarding the NCRRA project (a Thermo Electron subsidiary) constitute separate proceedings from those in which he participated as Executive Officer, and whether he can provide consulting services to Thermo Electron without violating the 'revolving door' provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request for an advice letter, not an FPPC response. No section headers present. The requestor references a prior FPPC letter from January 31, 1991 by John McLean regarding similar issues with the Delano Energy Company subsidiary. Document contains detailed factual background about CPCFA proceedings for NCRRA from 1985-1990.

embedding.summary: An incoming advice letter request from Douglas E. Chandler, former Executive Officer of the California Pollution Control Financing Authority (CPCFA), seeking confirmation that future CPCFA proceedings regarding the NCRRA project (a Thermo Electron subsidiary) are separate from prior proceedings in which he participated, and therefore do not violate the 'revolving door' provisions of the Political Reform Act if he provides consulting services.
